text: Upon entry with the laparoscopic camera, the surgeon manually reduces the majority of the incarcerated contents of the hernia, leaving only the descending colon drawn up with the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon observes a robust preperitoneal fat pad, noting that it was not mobilized with the anticipation that it would descend with the hernia sac.
The surgeon begins the preperitoneal dissection, carefully working to circumferentially dissect around the hernia defect at the neck of the hernia sac.
The surgeon performs a straightforward dissection of the lateral abdominal wall, carefully removing areolar tissue to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon approaches the medial margin of the hernia, with the rectus abdominis muscle visible. The hernia sac reduction process begins, focusing on separating and reducing the hernia contents.
The surgeon visually identifies the inferior and medial aspects of the hernia, utilizing two working arms for the operation.
The surgeon successfully reduces a large hernia sac, which is visually satisfying due to the absence of chronic adhesions, allowing for a straightforward reduction process.

The surgeon identifies the lateral margin of the hernia and begins invaginating the hernia sac to prevent it from falling back into the space. Cephalad dissection is performed to aid in the complete reduction of the hernia sac.

The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection around the myopectineal orifice on the patient's left side, carefully dissecting around the spermatic vessels. In the retropubic space, the direct hernia is visible.
The surgeon reduces the left direct hernia, ensuring adequate coverage of the Spigelian defect. The hernia sac is manipulated to prevent it from falling back into the surgical field.
The surgical field shows the reduced direct hernia on the left side, with a clear view of the Spigelian defect and the dissected myopectineal orifice. The right groin remains unaccessed.
The surgeon prepares for defect closure and mesh placement, selecting an eighteen-inch old permanent v-lock suture on a GS-XXII needle.

A port is visible, introduced just cephalad, initially intended for the placement of a large heavyweight mesh. However, overlapping mid-weight meshes are used instead, making the trocar placement likely unnecessary.

The surgeon places trocars strategically to ensure good mesh coverage, preventing trocar site hernias. Sequential tightening and traction are applied to the defect for closure. The insufflation is reduced to about ten to facilitate easy closure without tension.

The surgeon uses an eighteen-inch V-lock suture to close the defect, ensuring minimal suture usage. The mesh placement follows, with a midway extra-large mesh applied to the groin area, and barred soft mesh covering the retromuscular and preperitoneal spaces.
The surgeon places an extra large mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring broad coverage with at least ten centimeters extending in all directions to the midline.
The surgeon places multiple interrupted stitches to secure the mesh in position and applies fibrin sealant. The flap closure is performed near the hernia sac, where a small hole was previously made.

The surgeon has completed the exclusion of the mesh from the viscera, ensuring it is securely positioned. The large hernia sac is visible, and the trocar used for mesh introduction is withdrawn, allowing the preperitoneal air to escape.
